panels.title: Instance Information & Settings
panels.description: Core PostgreSQL configuration and status. Monitor version, uptime, and critical settings.
PostgreSQL version. Goal: Stay current with latest stable versions for security and performance. Action: Plan upgrades annually, test in staging first.
panels.title: Version

panels.description: Database uptime in days. Shows DOWN in red when pg_up=0.

targets.expr: ((pg_postmaster_start_time_seconds{job="$job", instance="$instance"} * 1000) * on(job, instance) pg_up{job="$job", instance="$instance"}) or on(job, instance) (pg_up{job="$job", instance="$instance"} * 0)

panels.title: Uptime

panels.description: Maximum allowed connections. Goal: Set appropriately for workload without over-provisioning. Action: If nearing limit, increase or investigate connection pooling.
panels.title: Max Connections

panels.description: Autovacuum enabled/disabled. Goal: Should be ON for production. Action: If OFF, enable immediately unless you have custom vacuum strategy.
panels.title: Autovacuum

panels.description: Fsync forces writes to disk. Goal: Should be ON for data safety. Action: If OFF, enable immediately unless you understand the risks.
panels.title: Fsync

panels.description: Synchronous commit enabled/disabled. Goal: Should be ON for data safety. Action: If OFF, understand performance vs durability tradeoff.
panels.title: Sync Commit

panels.description: Shared buffer cache size. Goal: 25% of RAM for dedicated DB server. Action: Tune based on working set size and available memory.
panels.title: Shared Buffers

panels.description: Work memory per query operation. Goal: Balance between parallelism and memory usage. Action: Increase for complex queries, monitor for memory pressure.
panels.title: Work Mem

panels.description: Maintenance work memory for vacuuming. Goal: Higher than work_mem for faster maintenance. Action: Increase if VACUUM/CREATE INDEX are slow.
panels.title: Maint Work Mem

panels.description: WAL buffer size. Goal: -1 (auto) or 1/32 of shared_buffers. Action: Usually default is fine, increase if high WAL write activity.
panels.title: WAL Buffers

panels.description: Checkpoint timeout in seconds. Goal: 5-15 minutes for OLTP. Action: Increase if checkpoints too frequent, decrease if recovery time concerns.
panels.title: Checkpoint Timeout

panels.description: Autovacuum max workers. Goal: 3-6 for most workloads. Action: Increase if tables not vacuumed frequently enough, monitor CPU usage.
panels.title: AV Max Workers

panels.description: Max locks per transaction. Goal: 64 default, increase for DDL-heavy workloads. Action: If hitting limit, increase and monitor memory usage.
panels.title: Max Locks/Tx

panels.description: Autovacuum naptime (sleep interval). Goal: 60s default is fine for most. Action: Reduce if tables need more frequent vacuuming.
panels.title: AV Naptime

panels.description: Minimum tuple insertions before analyze. Goal: 50 for small tables, higher for large. Action: Tune based on table size and update patterns.
panels.title: AV Analyze Threshold

panels.description: Minimum tuple updates/deletes before vacuum. Goal: 50 for small tables, higher for large. Action: Tune based on bloat tolerance.
panels.title: AV Vacuum Threshold
Critical Alerts - Connection Pool & Performance
panels.description: Key metrics requiring immediate attention when thresholds exceeded.

Used vs max connections (alert > 0.8). To see WHO holds the connections (by application/user/state), run in psql:

```sql
SELECT datname, usename, application_name, state, count(*) AS conns,
       max(round(extract(epoch FROM now()-state_change))::int) AS max_state_age_s
FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE backend_type='client backend'
GROUP BY datname, usename, application_name, state ORDER BY conns DESC;
```

Watch for large `idle in transaction` counts -- they hold locks. Without a pooler, cap app pool sizes or add pgbouncer; set idle_in_transaction_session_timeout.
panels.title: Connection Pool Utilization

panels.description: Connections in idle_in_transaction state - holding locks!
panels.title: Idle in Transaction (Dangerous!)

panels.description: Available vs Max connections
panels.title: Connection Pool Status

panels.description:
PostgreSQL performance degrades with **100+ ACTIVE connections**, regardless of max_connections setting.

**Common K8s Problem:**
- Pods autoscale -> connection pools multiply
- Result: Massive IDLE connections flooding the database
- Even with low active, total connections exhaust resources

**What to watch:**
- ACTIVE = Actual queries running (alert when >100)
- IDLE = Wasted connections from pod scaling
- IDLE IN TRANSACTION = Critical (holding locks)

**Solution:** Use PgBouncer for connection pooling in K8s

**Alert on:** Active > 100, NOT percentage utilization!
panels.title: Active vs Idle Connections

panels.description: Whether data page checksums are enabled on this cluster (pg_settings_data_checksums: green = on, orange = off). Checksums let PostgreSQL detect silent on-disk corruption; they were only enabled by default starting in PostgreSQL 18, so many older clusters have them OFF. This matters for reading the two checksum panels: when checksums are OFF the corruption counter can never increment, so a '0 failures' reading is NOT proof of a healthy disk - it just means detection is disabled. Green here means the 'Checksum Failures' panels are actually meaningful. Enabling checksums on an existing cluster requires pg_checksums with the server stopped (or a dump/restore).
panels.title: Data Checksums

panels.description: Total data-page checksum failures detected across the selected databases (pg_stat_database.checksums_failures). This is a hard corruption signal: any nonzero value (red) means PostgreSQL read a data page whose checksum did not match - i.e. silent storage corruption, a failing disk, or bad memory. It should be flat at 0 forever. If it rises: first confirm the 'Data Checksums' panel is green (otherwise the count is meaningless), then investigate hardware immediately, review server logs for the affected relation/block, and restore affected data from a known-good backup. Requires checksums enabled to ever fire (PG12+ for the counter).
panels.title: Checksum Failures Over Time

panels.description: Time since the most recent checksum failure was recorded (pg_stat_database.checksums_last_failure). Shows 'No failures' in neutral text when there has never been one - the healthy state. A red value (any age) means corruption was detected at that time; a recent age is more urgent than an old one, but any occurrence warrants investigating the underlying storage/hardware and validating backups. Read together with 'Checksum Failures Over Time' (the count) and 'Data Checksums' (whether detection is even on).
panels.title: Last Checksum Failure
Connection Analysis & Idle Age
panels.description: Connection state analysis - identify leaks, blocks, and connection pool issues.
Ranks databases by current idle connection pressure. Combines normal idle sessions with dangerous idle-in-transaction states so the worst offenders are obvious during incidents.
panels.title: Top Databases by Idle Connection State

panels.description: Connection breakdown by application (identify connection hogs)
panels.title: Connections by Application

panels.description: Idle connections by age - detect connection leaks
panels.title: Idle Connection Age Buckets

panels.description:
Age of the oldest long-running query. To see WHICH sessions are long-running or stuck idle-in-transaction (holding locks), run in psql:

```sql
SELECT pid, state, round(extract(epoch FROM now()-COALESCE(query_start,xact_start)),1) AS age_s,
       left(query,70) AS query
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE backend_type='client backend' AND pid<>pg_backend_pid()
  AND (state='active' OR state LIKE 'idle in transaction%')
  AND now()-COALESCE(query_start,xact_start) > interval '5 seconds'
ORDER BY age_s DESC;
```

Then `pg_cancel_backend(<pid>)` / `pg_terminate_backend(<pid>)`.
panels.title: Long-Running Query Age

panels.description:
Active connections by wait event type.

GOAL: Understand what database is waiting on
- Lock: Blocking queries, tune application
- IO: Slow disk, increase shared_buffers
- Client: Application not reading results fast
- CPU: Heavy processing, optimize queries

ACTIONS TO IMPROVE:
1. Lock waits: Enable log_lock_waits, reduce transaction time
2. IO waits: Add indexes, increase shared_buffers, faster storage
3. Client waits: Fix slow applications reading data
4. Check pg_stat_activity for wait_event details

QUERY: SELECT wait_event_type, wait_event, count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE wait_event IS NOT NULL GROUP BY 1,2;
panels.title: Wait Events

panels.description: Per-second rate of session lifecycle events from pg_stat_database (PG14+): new sessions established vs. sessions that were abandoned (client disconnected unexpectedly), fatal (terminated by a server error), or killed (terminated by an operator/admin). A low, steady establishment rate with near-zero abandoned/fatal/killed is healthy. A spike in sessions_abandoned or sessions_fatal is an early warning of connection-handling problems - app instances crashing or being OOM-killed, network drops, or aggressive proxy/pooler timeouts - often visible here before errors surface app-side. Especially useful for ECS/Kubernetes deployments that cycle instances: watch for churn spikes during rollouts. Per datname.
panels.title: Session Establishment & Termination Rate (PG14+)

panels.description: How the database's connection time is spent, from pg_stat_database (PG14+): total session_time accumulated per second vs. active_time (time actually executing queries) per second. The gap between them is time connections spend idle or idle-in-transaction. If session_time keeps climbing but active_time stays low, your connections are mostly sitting idle - a sign of an oversized or leaky connection pool, or long idle-in-transaction sessions (which also block VACUUM; see the Xmin Horizon panels). High active_time relative to session_time means connections are being used efficiently. Per datname.
panels.title: Session vs Active Time Rate (PG14+)

panels.description: Average lifetime of a database session (total session_time / number of sessions, PG14+). Interpretation depends on your architecture: a healthy pooled app holds long-lived connections, so large values mean good pool reuse. Conversely, if this suddenly drops toward zero while the establishment rate spikes (see 'Session Establishment & Termination Rate'), sessions are being created and torn down rapidly - connection churn from per-request connects, a misconfigured pool, or instances flapping during a deploy - which wastes backend startup cost and can exhaust max_connections. Per datname.
panels.title: Avg Session Duration (PG14+)

panels.description: Average write+sync work time per checkpoint (pg_stat_checkpointer write_time+sync_time divided by checkpoints completed). It must stay below checkpoint_timeout (yellow) — if it reaches the timeout the checkpointer cannot finish before the next checkpoint is due and is falling behind (storage bottleneck). Note: because checkpoint_completion_target defaults to 0.9, Postgres paces a timed checkpoint's writes over ~90% of the interval, so on a busy system it is normal for this to sit near ~0.9 x checkpoint_timeout; the red flag is crossing the timeout, not merely being high. This also excludes the log 'total=' value's interpretation. Requires PostgreSQL 17+.
panels.title: Avg Checkpoint Write+Sync Time per Checkpoint

panels.description: Seconds since the last completed checkpoint (pg_last_checkpoint_age_seconds). On a healthy primary this oscillates between 0 and checkpoint_timeout (yellow). A value climbing far past checkpoint_timeout indicates a stalled or severely lagging checkpointer. Works on all supported PostgreSQL versions.
panels.title: Time Since Last Checkpoint

panels.description: WAL bytes generated since the last checkpoint's redo point (pg_wal_bytes_since_last_checkpoint). This is also the amount of WAL replayed on crash recovery (RTO proxy). IMPORTANT: a WAL-driven (requested) checkpoint does NOT fire at max_wal_size (yellow) — Postgres triggers it at roughly max_wal_size / (2 + checkpoint_completion_target) (e.g. ~1/3 of max_wal_size) to leave room to spread checkpoint writes, so the peak plateaus well below the yellow line by design. Use it both ways: (1) INCREASE max_wal_size if the peak repeatedly plateaus around that ~1/3 point and the 'Checkpoints by Trigger' panel shows 'requested' dominating — checkpoints are WAL-driven, so a larger max_wal_size spaces them out (fewer checkpoints, fewer full-page images). Raising checkpoint_timeout alone will not help in that case. (2) DECREASE max_wal_size if the peak stays far below even that trigger and checkpoints are already 'timed' — it is oversized; shrink it to reclaim pg_wal disk and cap recovery volume, leaving margin above the observed peak. Caveat: a larger max_wal_size also means longer crash recovery (RTO). Works on all supported PostgreSQL versions.
panels.title: WAL Since Last Checkpoint (Recovery Volume)

panels.description: Rate of checkpoints by trigger. 'timed' = fired by checkpoint_timeout; 'requested' = fired by other causes, primarily WAL reaching max_wal_size (also manual CHECKPOINT). If 'requested' dominates, checkpoints are WAL-driven and you should tune max_wal_size, not just checkpoint_timeout. Requires PostgreSQL 17+.
panels.title: Checkpoints by Trigger (Timed vs Requested)
WAL
panels.description: Write-ahead log throughput, activity, buffer pressure, and full-page-image ratio.
WAL generation throughput in bytes/sec from pg_stat_wal.
panels.title: WAL Throughput

panels.description: WAL records/sec and full-page-image (FPI) generation/sec from pg_stat_wal.
panels.title: WAL Record Activity

panels.description: Times WAL writes were forced because WAL buffers became full (rate over 5m). Sustained non-zero values suggest WAL pressure.
panels.title: WAL Buffer Pressure

panels.description: Full-page-image share of WAL records (5m rate). Higher values indicate more WAL amplification, often around checkpoint pressure.
panels.title: FPI Ratio
Vacuum & Bloat Pressure (requires --collector.vacuum + --collector.stat)
panels.description: Predictive autovacuum pressure, dead tuple accumulation, estimated wasted space, freeze-age risk, and active vacuum execution. Use this row first for bloat diagnosis and repack candidates, then correlate with Table Statistics, Activity, and Database Size.
Ratio of dead tuples to the autovacuum trigger threshold. 1.0 means vacuum should trigger now, >1.0 means the table is overdue. This is the most useful early-warning panel for bloat pressure.
panels.title: Top Tables by Autovacuum Threshold Ratio

panels.description: Absolute dead tuple count by table. High values indicate vacuum debt and table churn. Use together with autovacuum threshold ratio to distinguish large healthy tables from overdue ones.
panels.title: Top Tables by Dead Tuples

panels.description: Stale-statistics risk normalized to each table's effective autoanalyze trigger. The collector divides n_mod_since_analyze by the configured threshold plus scale-factor component. 1.0 means autoanalyze should trigger now; >1.0 means planner statistics may be overdue.
panels.title: Stale Statistics: Autoanalyze Threshold Ratio

panels.description: Time since the last automatic vacuum and analyze by table. Large values on write-heavy tables suggest maintenance is not keeping up or is blocked. Tables that have never been autoanalyzed have no age series and are counted by the adjacent Never Autoanalyzed Tables panel.
panels.title: Tables Since Last Automatic Maintenance

panels.description: Database freeze age as a percentage of autovacuum_freeze_max_age. Rising values indicate wraparound risk and make vacuum health a database-level concern, not just a table-level one.
panels.title: Database Freeze Age (% of Max)

panels.description: Number of tables that have never been autovacuumed. Should usually trend to 0 except for very new or static tables.
panels.title: Never Autovacuumed Tables

panels.description: Number of tables that have never been autoanalyzed. Persistent non-zero values can indicate stale planner stats risk.
panels.title: Never Autoanalyzed Tables

panels.description:
Top repack candidates by estimated dead space. To list tables by dead tuples / dead %, with size and last (auto)vacuum, run in psql:

```sql
SELECT schemaname, relname, n_dead_tup, n_live_tup,
       CASE WHEN n_live_tup+n_dead_tup>0 THEN round(100.0*n_dead_tup/(n_live_tup+n_dead_tup),1) ELSE 0 END AS dead_pct,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(relid)) AS table_size, last_autovacuum
FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE n_dead_tup>0 ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC LIMIT 25;
```

Act: VACUUM (VERBOSE, ANALYZE) to reclaim for reuse, or pg_repack / VACUUM FULL to return space to the OS (VACUUM FULL takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE -> maintenance window).
panels.title: Top Repack Candidates by Estimated Dead Space

panels.description: Largest tables by on-disk size. Use together with bloat ratio and estimated dead space to prioritize which large tables are worth repacking first.
panels.title: Top Tables by Table Size

panels.description:
Progress of currently running vacuum operations (0.0-1.0 ratio). Useful only while a manual vacuum or autovacuum is actively running. If this panel is empty and vacuum pressure panels are high, vacuum is not currently catching up.

The exporter resolves table labels by querying each database locally, so the panel can show `database` and `schema.table` labels across the cluster. For manual psql diagnosis, connect to the target database shown in the panel before joining `pg_stat_progress_vacuum` to `pg_class`:

```sql
SELECT
    current_database() AS database,
    n.nspname || '.' || c.relname AS table,
    p.phase,
    p.heap_blks_scanned || '/' || p.heap_blks_total AS heap_progress,
    p.index_vacuum_count
FROM pg_stat_progress_vacuum p
LEFT JOIN pg_class c ON p.relid = c.oid
LEFT JOIN pg_namespace n ON n.oid = c.relnamespace;
```

**What is a good state?**
Ideally, vacuums should run quickly and disappear from this panel. If you see a vacuum stuck at the same percentage for a long time (especially in the `scanning heap` or `vacuuming indexes` phases), it may be blocked by a long-running transaction holding an old snapshot, or it may be suffering from slow disk I/O. Check your lock and activity panels to see if anything is blocking it!
panels.title: Active Vacuum Progress

panels.description: Estimated bloat ratio derived from dead tuples versus total tuples. 0.5 means roughly 50% of tuples are dead, which is a practical threshold for tables that deserve immediate review for vacuum tuning or repack planning.
panels.title: Top Tables by Estimated Bloat Ratio

panels.description: Age, in transaction IDs (XIDs), of the oldest xmin snapshot each holder type is pinning: backend (a live session, often 'idle in transaction'), prepared_xact (an open two-phase transaction), replication_slot (a slot's xmin or catalog_xmin). Whichever line is highest is what currently stops VACUUM from reclaiming dead rows and holds back transaction-ID wraparound protection. Flat and low is healthy; a single line climbing steadily is the problem to chase - a long-lived transaction, an orphaned prepared xact, or a stale/lagging replication slot. Rule of thumb: start worrying as this approaches autovacuum_freeze_max_age (default 200,000,000), and note the cluster stops accepting writes near 2,000,000,000 to prevent wraparound. Use the 'Worst Xmin Horizon Holder' panel below to see exactly which session, gid, or slot to act on.
panels.title: Xmin Horizon Age by Holder (XIDs)

panels.description: Count of open prepared (two-phase / 2PC) transactions left in PREPARE TRANSACTION state, waiting for COMMIT PREPARED or ROLLBACK PREPARED. Fewer is better: 0 (green) is the healthy normal for almost all systems, since 2PC is only used by specific distributed-transaction / XA setups. Each open prepared xact pins the xmin horizon and holds its locks indefinitely until resolved, so even one that lingers blocks vacuum and drives bloat and transaction-ID wraparound pressure. 1 or more (yellow) that persists for more than a few minutes usually means an application or transaction manager crashed mid-2PC and orphaned it; 5 or more (red) points at a systemic problem such as a broken transaction coordinator or a misconfigured app. If your workload does not use 2PC/XA at all, this should always be 0 and any nonzero value is suspicious. To fix: find the gid in the 'Worst Xmin Horizon Holder' table, and after confirming with the app owner run ROLLBACK PREPARED '<gid>' (or COMMIT PREPARED).
panels.title: Prepared Transactions

panels.description: How long the oldest open prepared (two-phase) transaction has been sitting unresolved, in seconds. It should normally be empty or 0 - a prepared xact is meant to be committed or rolled back within milliseconds. Green = none or very recent; yellow (over 5 minutes) = something forgot to finish a two-phase commit; red (over 1 hour) = an orphaned prepared xact is almost certainly pinning the xmin horizon and blocking VACUUM (cross-check 'Prepared Transactions' and 'Xmin Horizon Age by Holder'). The longer it runs, the more bloat and wraparound pressure it causes. Fix by resolving it: ROLLBACK PREPARED '<gid>' (or COMMIT PREPARED) after confirming with the app owner; the gid is shown in 'Worst Xmin Horizon Holder'.
panels.title: Oldest Prepared Xact Age (s)

panels.description: The single worst offender per holder type, labelled with its identity so you know exactly what to act on: backend -> application_name (terminate with pg_terminate_backend(pid)), prepared_xact -> gid (resolve with ROLLBACK PREPARED '<gid>' or COMMIT PREPARED), replication_slot -> slot_name (drop with pg_drop_replication_slot or let the consumer catch up). The value is that holder's xmin age in XIDs - the higher it is, the more it is holding VACUUM back. Empty or low is healthy. When 'Xmin Horizon Age by Holder' above is climbing, this panel names the culprit: match the holder label to the matching action. Reset every scrape, so a resolved holder disappears immediately.
panels.title: Worst Xmin Horizon Holder (XIDs)

panels.description: Live block-level progress of CREATE INDEX, CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY, and REINDEX operations (pg_stat_progress_create_index). Series appear only while a build is running and vanish when it finishes, so an empty panel is normal - it lights up during migrations or index maintenance. Use it to confirm a long index build is actually advancing (blocks_done rising toward blocks_total) rather than stuck waiting on a lock, and to estimate how long a big-table migration will take. If the value stalls, a CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY is likely blocked waiting for conflicting transactions to finish - the Locks and 'Xmin Horizon Age by Holder' panels show what is in the way. Bounded cardinality: one series per in-flight build.
panels.title: Active CREATE INDEX Progress

panels.description: Live progress of ANALYZE sampling blocks scanned (pg_stat_progress_analyze). Series appear only while ANALYZE (manual or autovacuum-triggered) is running, so an empty panel is normal. Watch sample_blks_scanned rise toward the total to confirm statistics collection on a large table is progressing - stale planner statistics after a bulk load or migration cause bad query plans, and this shows the refresh happening. A long-stalled ANALYZE on a huge table can delay a deploy; if it is not advancing, check for heavy write contention or lock waits. Bounded cardinality: one series per in-flight ANALYZE.
panels.title: Active ANALYZE Progress (sample blocks)
Vacuum Horizon Blockers & Progress (requires --collector.vacuum)
panels.description: What is pinning the xmin horizon (backends, prepared transactions, replication slots) and live CREATE INDEX / ANALYZE progress.

Top queries by total execution time since reset. To drill in from psql:

```sql
SELECT round((total_exec_time/1000)::numeric,1) AS total_s, calls,
       round(mean_exec_time::numeric,2) AS mean_ms, rows,
       left(query,70) AS query
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC LIMIT 20;
```

High mean_ms + low calls = a slow query; low mean_ms + huge calls = run too often. Requires pg_stat_statements.
panels.title: Top 10 Queries by Total Execution Time Since Reset

panels.description: Queries with the highest average execution time per call from the current pg_stat_statements snapshot
panels.title: Top 10 Slowest Queries (Mean Time)

panels.description: Queries with the highest recent execution rate over the last 5 minutes
panels.title: Query Call Rate (per second)

panels.description: Queries generating the most cumulative WAL since the last pg_stat_statements reset
panels.title: WAL Generation by Query Since Reset

panels.description: Queries with cumulative temp block usage since the last pg_stat_statements reset; sustained growth usually indicates spill-heavy plans
panels.title: Queries Spilling to Disk Since Reset

panels.description: Queries whose current snapshot cache hit ratio is below 90%
panels.title: Queries with Low Cache Hit Ratio (<90%)

options.content:
### How to use the `queryid`
When you notice a query consuming high resources (CPU, time, or disk), the legend will show its truncated form followed by its `queryid`, for example:
`postgres | SELECT * FROM users WHERE... | 1234567890`

You can use that `queryid` (`1234567890`) to retrieve the full, un-truncated query text directly from your database.

**Instructions:**
1. Copy the `queryid` from the legend of the problematic query.
2. Connect to your PostgreSQL cluster using `psql`, pgAdmin, DBeaver, or your preferred SQL client.
3. Run the following query, replacing `<queryid from the legend>` with your copied ID:

```sql
SELECT 
    d.datname AS database, 
    s.calls, 
    round(s.mean_exec_time/1000) AS mean_s, 
    round(s.max_exec_time/60000) AS max_min, 
    s.query 
FROM pg_stat_statements s 
JOIN pg_database d ON d.oid = s.dbid 
WHERE s.queryid = <queryid from the legend>;
```

> **Note:** This works from any database on the cluster because the `dbid` join resolves the correct database name. The `queryid` is stable for a statement's shape but may change across PostgreSQL major version upgrades.

panels.title: How to Look Up Full Queries
Query Performance (requires --collector.statements)
panels.description: Top resource-consuming queries. Requires pg_stat_statements extension. Optimize these first for maximum impact.
Queries that added the most cumulative execution time during the last 5 minutes
panels.title: Top 10 Queries by DB Time Added (Last 5m)

panels.description: Average execution time per call during the last 5 minutes; useful for catching newly slow queries
panels.title: Top 10 Queries by Mean Time (Last 5m)

panels.description: Queries with the highest recent execution rate over the last 5 minutes
panels.title: Top 10 Queries by Call Rate (Last 5m)

panels.description: Queries that generated the most WAL during the last 5 minutes
panels.title: Top 10 Queries by WAL Added (Last 5m)

panels.description: Queries that added the most temp block reads or writes during the last 5 minutes
panels.title: Top 10 Queries by Temp Blocks Added (Last 5m)

panels.description: Queries whose current snapshot cache hit ratio is below 90%; pair this with the recent-pressure panels to identify active disk-heavy queries
panels.title: Queries with Low Cache Hit Ratio (Current Snapshot)
Recent Query Pressure (last 5m, requires --collector.statements)
panels.description: Recent query pressure for incident response. These panels focus on activity observed in the last 5 minutes rather than lifetime totals since pg_stat_statements_reset().
Active database connections and queries per second
panels.title: Active Connections & Query Rate

panels.description: Active connections by database and state
panels.title: Connections by Database & State

panels.description: Connections waiting or blocked by locks
panels.title: Waiting & Blocked Connections

panels.description: Database disk I/O operations
panels.title: Database Disk Reads

panels.description: DML operations per second by database
panels.title: Transaction Throughput (INS/UPD/DEL)

panels.description:
Checkpoint frequency and timing.

REQUIRES: PostgreSQL 17+ (uses pg_stat_checkpointer)
For PostgreSQL 16 and earlier, this panel will show no data.

GOAL: Checkpoints spread evenly, meeting checkpoint_completion_target
- Too frequent: Tune checkpoint_timeout, increase max_wal_size
- Too slow: Increase shared_buffers

ACTIONS TO IMPROVE:
1. Tune checkpoint_timeout (5min-30min typical)
2. Set checkpoint_completion_target = 0.9
3. Increase max_wal_size for write-heavy workloads
4. Monitor checkpoint_warning in logs
5. Balance I/O load distribution

METRICS: Timed vs requested checkpoints (from pg_stat_checkpointer)
panels.title: Checkpoint Activity

panels.description:
WAL archiving status (if configured).

GOAL: All archives successful, no failures
- Failed archives: Check archive_command
- Growing failed count: Storage or network issues

ACTIONS TO IMPROVE:
1. Verify archive_command is correct
2. Check archive destination has space
3. Test network connectivity to archive location
4. Monitor archive_status directory growth
5. Set archive_timeout for streaming

METRICS: Archived count vs failed count
panels.title: WAL Archiver Status

panels.description:
Rate of tuples fetched by queries.

GOAL: Monitor read activity patterns
- High fetch rates: Heavy read workload
- Sudden spikes: Investigate query patterns

METRICS: Tuples fetched per second by database
panels.title: Tuples Fetched Rate

panels.description:
Rate of tuples inserted into tables.

GOAL: Monitor write activity patterns
- High insert rates: Heavy ingestion workload
- Growing trends: Capacity planning needed

METRICS: Tuples inserted per second by database
panels.title: Tuples Inserted Rate

panels.description:
Rate of tuples updated in tables.

GOAL: Monitor update activity patterns
- High update rates: Heavy modification workload
- Compare with deletes: Data lifecycle insights

METRICS: Tuples updated per second by database
panels.title: Tuples Updated Rate

panels.description:
Total tuples fetched since PostgreSQL restart.

GOAL: Track total read activity
- Shows cumulative fetch operations
- Resets on PostgreSQL restart
- Use for workload analysis

METRICS: Sum of all tuples fetched across databases
panels.title: Total Tuples Fetched

panels.description:
Total tuples inserted since PostgreSQL restart.

GOAL: Track total data ingestion
- Shows cumulative insert activity
- Resets on PostgreSQL restart
- Use for capacity planning

METRICS: Sum of all tuples inserted across databases
panels.title: Total Tuples Inserted

panels.description:
Total tuples updated since PostgreSQL restart.

GOAL: Track total update activity
- Shows cumulative update operations
- Resets on PostgreSQL restart
- Use for workload analysis

METRICS: Sum of all tuples updated across databases
panels.title: Total Tuples Updated
Database Activity & I/O (requires --collector.database)
panels.description: Write-ahead log, buffer activity, and I/O patterns. Monitor for performance bottlenecks.
Lock counts by database and lock mode
panels.title: Lock Count by Database & Mode

panels.description: Sessions waiting on a lock (blocked) vs sessions holding locks that block others (blocking). Sustained >0 means lock contention is stalling work.
panels.title: Blocked vs Blocking Sessions

panels.description:
Age of the longest currently-blocked session. High/red means a query is stuck waiting on a lock. To find WHO/WHAT is blocking, run in psql:

```sql
SELECT blocked.pid AS blocked_pid, left(blocked.query,50) AS blocked_query,
       blocking.pid AS blocking_pid, left(blocking.query,60) AS blocking_query,
       blocking.state AS blocking_state
FROM pg_stat_activity blocked
JOIN LATERAL unnest(pg_blocking_pids(blocked.pid)) AS bp(pid) ON true
JOIN pg_stat_activity blocking ON blocking.pid = bp.pid
WHERE cardinality(pg_blocking_pids(blocked.pid)) > 0
ORDER BY now()-blocked.query_start DESC;
```

Then: `SELECT pg_terminate_backend(<blocking_pid>);` or fix the root cause (long/idle-in-transaction sessions, slow UPDATEs from a missing index, blocking DDL).
panels.title: Longest Lock Wait

panels.description: Total sessions currently waiting on a lock across selected databases.
panels.title: Blocked Sessions (total)

panels.description: Locks requested but not yet granted, broken down by lock mode. Identifies WHICH lock type is contended (e.g. RowExclusiveLock, AccessExclusiveLock).
panels.title: Ungranted Lock Waits by Mode
Locks & Blocking (requires --collector.locks)
panels.description: Lock monitoring and blocking query detection. Goal: minimal blocking, no deadlocks.

Replication lag in seconds. 

VALUES:
- 0: Primary or caught-up replica
- >0: Delay behind primary

Use pg_replication_is_replica to distinguish server role.
panels.title: Replication Lag

panels.description: Indicates if the server is a replica (1) or primary (0).
panels.title: Server Role

panels.description: Maximum WAL lag in bytes across connected replicas. Useful to spot backlog growth and sustained replica delay.
panels.title: Max Replication Lag Bytes

panels.description: Rate at which logical decoding is spilling in-progress transaction data to disk (pg_stat_replication_slots.spill_bytes) because a transaction exceeded logical_decoding_work_mem before it could be streamed. 0 is ideal. Sustained spilling (yellow) means a subscriber is decoding large or long transactions and paying extra disk I/O and CPU - a common cause of logical replication lag. To reduce it: raise logical_decoding_work_mem, break up large transactions, or enable streaming of in-progress transactions (PG14+). Broken down per slot_name so you can see which subscription is responsible.
panels.title: Logical Slot Spill to Disk (bytes/s)

panels.description: Rate at which logical decoding streamed in-progress (not-yet-committed) transaction data straight to the subscriber (pg_stat_replication_slots.stream_bytes), a PG14+ optimization that avoids spilling large transactions to disk. Seeing streaming instead of spilling (see 'Logical Slot Spill to Disk') is the healthier outcome for big transactions. This is informational: high values simply mean large transactions are being replicated efficiently rather than hitting disk. Per slot_name.
panels.title: Logical Slot Streamed to Subscriber (bytes/s)

panels.description: Rate of transactions that logical decoding had to spill to disk vs. stream to the subscriber (pg_stat_replication_slots.spill_txns / stream_txns). Rising spill_txns is the warning sign - each spilled transaction was too big for logical_decoding_work_mem and hit disk, slowing replication; stream_txns rising instead is the efficient PG14+ path. If spill dominates, raise logical_decoding_work_mem or reduce transaction size. Pairs with the bytes/s panels to the left. Per slot_name.
panels.title: Logical Slot Spill / Stream Transactions (rate)
Replication (requires --collector.replication)
panels.description: Replication lag and role. Monitor replica health and streaming replication status.

Rate of INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE operations per second.

 REQUIRES: --collector.stat flag to be enabled

GOAL: Monitor for patterns, optimize high-activity tables
- High INSERT rate: Consider bulk loading, COPY
- High UPDATE rate: May cause bloat, check HOT updates
- High DELETE rate: Triggers vacuum, monitor bloat

ACTIONS TO IMPROVE:
1. Batch operations when possible
2. Use COPY instead of INSERT for bulk data
3. Enable HOT updates (avoid updating indexed columns)
4. Partition high-activity tables
5. Tune autovacuum for write-heavy tables
6. Monitor table_size growth

WHY SHOWING 'No Data':
1.  --collector.stat NOT enabled (most common cause)
2. No write activity in selected databases
3. Check database filter ($database variable)
4. Metrics are rate(counter[5m]) - needs sustained activity over 5 minutes

TO FIX: Start pg_exporter with --collector.stat flag
panels.title: Table DML Operations Rate

panels.description:
Avg rows read per sequential scan (missing-index signal). To list the actual candidate tables, run in psql:

```sql
SELECT schemaname, relname, seq_scan, seq_tup_read,
       CASE WHEN seq_scan>0 THEN seq_tup_read/seq_scan ELSE 0 END AS avg_rows_per_scan,
       CASE WHEN seq_scan+idx_scan>0 THEN round(100.0*idx_scan/(seq_scan+idx_scan),1) END AS idx_use_pct,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(relid)) AS table_size
FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE seq_scan>0 ORDER BY seq_tup_read DESC LIMIT 25;
```

Tables high in seq_tup_read with low idx_use_pct and large size are the strongest index candidates. Confirm the query with EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS), then CREATE INDEX.
panels.title: Avg Rows Read per Sequential Scan (missing-index signal)

panels.description: idx_scan / (idx_scan + seq_scan) per table. A low ratio on a large, frequently-scanned table suggests a missing or unused index. 1.0 = always index access.

targets.expr: pg_stat_user_tables_idx_scan{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"} / clamp_min(pg_stat_user_tables_idx_scan{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"} + pg_stat_user_tables_seq_scan{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"}, 1)

panels.title: Index-Usage Ratio (lower = more seq scans)

panels.description: Per-second rate of sequential scans per table (deriv over the cumulative gauge; deriv is used instead of rate because the exporter publishes these pg_stat_user_tables counters as gauges). Spikes on large tables point at queries scanning instead of using an index.
panels.title: Sequential Scan Rate (per second)
Table Cache Hit Ratio
panels.description:
To drill in and find which tables have the worst cache hit ratio or highest disk I/O, run in psql:

```sql
SELECT relname,
       heap_blks_read,
       heap_blks_hit,
       round(heap_blks_hit::numeric / nullif(heap_blks_hit + heap_blks_read, 0), 4) AS hit_ratio
FROM pg_statio_user_tables
ORDER BY heap_blks_read DESC LIMIT 10;
```

**What is a good value?**
A healthy cache hit ratio is typically **> 0.99 (99%)**. If your ratio consistently drops below 0.95, it indicates that PostgreSQL is frequently reading from disk because active table data does not fit in memory. You may need to increase `shared_buffers` or provision more RAM.

targets.expr: sum by (datname) (rate(pg_stat_user_tables_heap_blks_hit_total{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"}[$__rate_interval])) / (sum by (datname) (rate(pg_stat_user_tables_heap_blks_hit_total{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"}[$__rate_interval])) + sum by (datname) (rate(pg_stat_user_tables_heap_blks_read_total{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"}[$__rate_interval])))

panels.title: Table Block I/O (Hits vs Reads)
panels.description:
To drill in and find which tables have the worst cache hit ratio or highest disk I/O, run in psql:

```sql
SELECT relname,
       heap_blks_read,
       heap_blks_hit,
       round(heap_blks_hit::numeric / nullif(heap_blks_hit + heap_blks_read, 0), 4) AS hit_ratio
FROM pg_statio_user_tables
ORDER BY heap_blks_read DESC LIMIT 10;
```

**What is a good value?**
A healthy cache hit ratio is typically **> 0.99 (99%)**. If your ratio consistently drops below 0.95, it indicates that PostgreSQL is frequently reading from disk because active table data does not fit in memory. You may need to increase `shared_buffers` or provision more RAM.

Estimated live rows per table from PostgreSQL statistics. This is a lightweight estimate rather than an exact COUNT(*); use it to identify the largest row populations and interpret scan, bloat, and maintenance signals.
panels.title: Top Tables by Estimated Row Count (Live Tuples)

panels.description: Windowed HOT updates divided by total updates for tables with update activity. Low sustained values mean PostgreSQL is rewriting indexes for most updates; check whether indexed columns are changing and whether a lower fillfactor would leave enough page space. Some workloads are not HOT-eligible, so use this as an investigation signal rather than a universal target.

targets.expr: bottomk(15, (clamp_min(delta(pg_stat_user_tables_n_tup_hot_upd{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"}[$__rate_interval]), 0) / clamp_min(delta(pg_stat_user_tables_n_tup_upd{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"}[$__rate_interval]), 0.000000001)) and on(datname, schemaname, relname) (clamp_min(delta(pg_stat_user_tables_n_tup_upd{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"}[$__rate_interval]), 0) > 0))

panels.title: HOT Update Ratio (Active Tables)

panels.description: Total disk space used by all indexes attached to each table. Large index footprints increase cache pressure, write amplification, and vacuum work, but can be legitimate for large or read-heavy tables. Compare with table size and the unused-index panels before dropping anything.
panels.title: Top Tables by Total Index Size

panels.description: Windowed shared-buffer hit ratio for index blocks, ranked from lowest to highest and limited to tables with observed index I/O. Reads indicate blocks not found in shared_buffers and may still be served by the operating-system cache. Investigate sustained low ratios together with read volume, query latency, and index size.

targets.expr: bottomk(15, (clamp_min(delta(pg_stat_user_tables_idx_blks_hit_total{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"}[$__rate_interval]), 0) / clamp_min(clamp_min(delta(pg_stat_user_tables_idx_blks_hit_total{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"}[$__rate_interval]), 0) + clamp_min(delta(pg_stat_user_tables_idx_blks_read_total{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"}[$__rate_interval]), 0), 0.000000001)) and on(datname, schemaname, relname) ((clamp_min(delta(pg_stat_user_tables_idx_blks_hit_total{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"}[$__rate_interval]), 0) + clamp_min(delta(pg_stat_user_tables_idx_blks_read_total{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"}[$__rate_interval]), 0)) > 0))

panels.title: Lowest Active Table Index Cache Hit Ratio

panels.description: Windowed live rows fetched divided by index scans for tables with scan activity. High values can indicate broad range scans or low selectivity, but may be expected for legitimate batch queries and bitmap scans. Confirm the responsible SQL and execution plan before changing an index.

targets.expr: topk(15, (clamp_min(delta(pg_stat_user_tables_idx_tup_fetch{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"}[$__rate_interval]), 0) / clamp_min(delta(pg_stat_user_tables_idx_scan{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"}[$__rate_interval]), 0.000000001)) and on(datname, schemaname, relname) (clamp_min(delta(pg_stat_user_tables_idx_scan{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"}[$__rate_interval]), 0) > 0))

panels.title: Rows Fetched per Active Index Scan
Table Statistics (requires --collector.stat)
panels.description: Table-level DML, cardinality, HOT-update efficiency, scan selectivity, index footprint, and heap/index cache behavior. Identify hot tables and optimization candidates. REQUIRES --collector.stat flag.

Total size of each database in bytes.

GOAL: Monitor growth trends, plan capacity
- Sudden growth: investigate unexpected data
- Steady growth: normal, plan storage scaling

ACTIONS TO IMPROVE:
1. Identify large tables (pg_total_relation_size)
2. Archive old data
3. Partition large tables
4. Drop unused indexes
5. Run VACUUM FULL on bloated tables (maintenance window)
6. Consider table compression (TOAST settings)
panels.title: Database Size

panels.description:
Database size change rate in bytes/sec.

GOAL: Predictable, steady growth
- Spikes: Investigate bulk loads or bloat
- Negative: VACUUM FULL or DROP operations

ACTIONS TO IMPROVE:
1. Implement data retention policies
2. Archive historical data
3. Monitor autovacuum effectiveness
4. Plan storage capacity 6-12 months ahead
5. Use partitioning for time-series data
panels.title: Database Growth Rate
Database Size & Growth (requires --collector.database)
panels.description: Database and table size trends. Plan capacity and identify growth patterns.

Cumulative total of index scans since PostgreSQL started, broken down per database (datname).

GOAL: Should be steadily increasing
- Growing: Indexes are being used
- Flat/zero: Unused indexes (candidates for removal)

ACTIONS TO IMPROVE:
1. Identify tables with high sequential scans
2. Analyze query patterns with EXPLAIN
3. Create missing indexes for tables with seq scans
4. Drop unused indexes to reduce write overhead

NOTE: This is a cumulative counter, not a rate. Resets on PostgreSQL restart. One line per database (datname), following the Database selector (All = every database); use sum() for a single cluster total. Enable --collector.index to get this metric.
panels.title: Index Scans by Database

panels.description:
Total size of user indexes in bytes, broken down per database (datname).

GOAL: Monitor index storage usage
- Growing: Normal with data growth
- Large size: May indicate bloat or unused indexes

ACTIONS TO IMPROVE:
1. Identify and drop unused indexes
2. REINDEX bloated indexes (maintenance window)
3. Review duplicate or redundant indexes
4. Monitor after index changes

NOTE: One line per database (datname), following the Database selector (All = every database); use sum() for a single cluster total. Enable --collector.index to get this metric.
panels.title: Index Size by Database
Index Cache Hit Ratio
panels.description:
To drill in and find which indexes have the worst cache hit ratio or highest disk I/O, run in psql:

```sql
SELECT relname AS table_name,
       indexrelname AS index_name,
       idx_blks_read,
       idx_blks_hit,
       round(idx_blks_hit::numeric / nullif(idx_blks_hit + idx_blks_read, 0), 4) AS hit_ratio
FROM pg_statio_user_indexes
ORDER BY idx_blks_read DESC LIMIT 10;
```

**What is a good value?**
A healthy index cache hit ratio is typically **> 0.99 (99%)**. Indexes are critical for fast lookups and should ideally reside entirely in memory. If this ratio drops below 0.95, queries will suffer significant disk I/O latency. Consider increasing `shared_buffers`, adding RAM, or dropping unused/duplicate indexes to free up cache space.

targets.expr: sum by (datname) (rate(pg_index_idx_blks_hit_total{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"}[$__rate_interval])) / (sum by (datname) (rate(pg_index_idx_blks_hit_total{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"}[$__rate_interval])) + sum by (datname) (rate(pg_index_idx_blks_read_total{job="$job", instance="$instance", datname=~"$database"}[$__rate_interval])))

panels.title: Index Block I/O (Hits vs Reads)
panels.description:
To drill in and find which indexes have the worst cache hit ratio or highest disk I/O, run in psql:

```sql
SELECT relname AS table_name,
       indexrelname AS index_name,
       idx_blks_read,
       idx_blks_hit,
       round(idx_blks_hit::numeric / nullif(idx_blks_hit + idx_blks_read, 0), 4) AS hit_ratio
FROM pg_statio_user_indexes
ORDER BY idx_blks_read DESC LIMIT 10;
```

**What is a good value?**
A healthy index cache hit ratio is typically **> 0.99 (99%)**. Indexes are critical for fast lookups and should ideally reside entirely in memory. If this ratio drops below 0.95, queries will suffer significant disk I/O latency. Consider increasing `shared_buffers`, adding RAM, or dropping unused/duplicate indexes to free up cache space.

panels.title: Index Performance & Unused Indexes (requires --collector.index)
panels.description: Index usage statistics and unused index detection. Optimize by adding missing indexes and dropping unused ones.
Shows whether SSL/TLS is enabled on PostgreSQL server. Goal: Should be 1 (enabled) for production systems. Action: Enable SSL in postgresql.conf if disabled.
panels.title: SSL Status

panels.description: Certificate validity status. Goal: Should be 1 (valid). Action: Renew certificate if invalid or expiring soon.
panels.title: Certificate Status

panels.description: Days until SSL certificate expires. Goal: Keep above 30 days. Critical: Below 7 days. Action: Renew certificates before expiration to avoid service disruption.
panels.title: Certificate Expiry

panels.description: Number of active SSL/TLS encrypted connections. Goal: All connections should use SSL in production. Action: Configure clients to use SSL and set ssl=on in pg_hba.conf.
panels.title: Active SSL Connections

panels.description: Active SSL connections grouped by TLS protocol version. Best: TLSv1.3 (modern, faster, more secure). Acceptable: TLSv1.2. Deprecated/Insecure: TLSv1.0 and TLSv1.1 (disabled in modern browsers since 2020). Action: If old TLS versions appear, identify and upgrade those clients immediately.
panels.title: SSL Connections by TLS Version

panels.description: Active SSL connections grouped by cipher suite. Goal: Use strong ciphers (AES-256-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305). Avoid: RC4, 3DES, CBC-mode ciphers (vulnerable to BEAST/Lucky13 attacks), and export-grade ciphers. Action: Review ssl_ciphers in postgresql.conf and ensure ssl_prefer_server_ciphers=on.
panels.title: SSL Connections by Cipher Suite

panels.description: Average encryption key strength (bits) across all active SSL/TLS connections. Common strengths: 128-bit (AES-128-GCM, older ciphers), 256-bit (AES-256-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305 - preferred for modern security). Goal: 256 bits for production. Warning: Below 128 bits indicates weak/deprecated ciphers. Action: If average is low, check 'SSL Connections by Cipher Suite' panel and upgrade client TLS configurations.
panels.title: Average Encryption Strength

panels.description: SSL certificate validity period showing not_before and not_after timestamps. Goal: Ensure certificate is within validity period and track expiration. Action: Plan certificate renewal before expiration.
panels.title: Certificate Validity Period
TLS/SSL Security & Encryption (requires --collector.tls)
panels.description: Monitor SSL/TLS certificate expiration, encryption strength, and connection security. Enable with --collector.tls flag.

Active client backends NOT waiting on any event = running on CPU. Compare the total to the instance vCPU count: sustained >= vCPUs means CPU saturation. To find WHICH queries are burning CPU, run in psql:

```sql
SELECT pid, datname, round(extract(epoch FROM now()-query_start),1) AS running_s,
       left(query,80) AS query
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE state='active' AND wait_event IS NULL
  AND backend_type='client backend' AND pid<>pg_backend_pid()
ORDER BY query_start;
```

Then: `SELECT pg_cancel_backend(<pid>);` (gentle) or `pg_terminate_backend(<pid>)`. For cumulative CPU hogs, check pg_stat_statements by total_exec_time (Query Performance row). A common root cause is a missing index causing seq scans.
panels.title: On-CPU Backends by Database

panels.description: Sum of active, non-waiting backends. Compare to vCPU count; >= vCPUs indicates CPU saturation.
panels.title: Total On-CPU Backends
I/O by backend type — requires --collector.stat_io
panels.description: Cluster-wide backend I/O from pg_stat_io (PostgreSQL 16+), broken down by backend_type, object and context. Surfaces shared_buffers pressure (evictions), cache effectiveness (hits vs reads) and storage latency (read/write/fsync time) directly from inside PostgreSQL — no host access needed, so it works on managed services (RDS/Aurora). REQUIRES --collector.stat_io; latency panels also require track_io_timing = on.

Cluster-wide rate of buffer evictions (pg_stat_io.evictions): pages pushed out of shared_buffers to make room for new reads. This is the most direct signal of shared_buffers pressure. Low/green is healthy; climbing into yellow/orange/red means the working set no longer fits in shared_buffers and PostgreSQL is churning the cache - expect more disk reads, a lower cache hit ratio, and higher query latency. If sustained high: consider increasing shared_buffers, or find what is scanning large amounts of data (sequential scans, big reporting queries). Especially valuable on managed services (RDS/Aurora) where you cannot see host-level I/O. Requires --collector.stat_io (PG16+).
panels.title: Eviction Rate

panels.description: Fraction of block requests served from shared_buffers instead of disk (hits / (hits + reads)), derived from pg_stat_io. Green (over 99%) is the goal for OLTP workloads. Dropping into yellow/red means queries go to disk (or the OS page cache) far more often, usually because shared_buffers is too small for the working set or a large scan is evicting hot pages (see 'Eviction Rate'). A persistent low ratio correlates directly with higher latency. Note: reads counted here may still be served by the OS cache, so this is PostgreSQL's buffer-cache view, not necessarily physical disk. PG16+.
panels.title: Buffer Cache Hit Ratio

panels.description: Cluster-wide bytes/sec read by PostgreSQL (pg_stat_io read_bytes) across all backend types and contexts. Use it to size real read demand and to correlate latency spikes with read bursts (e.g. a big analytical query or a cache-cold period after restart). High sustained reads alongside a falling cache hit ratio and rising evictions indicate memory pressure rather than just a busy-but-cached workload. Great for managed databases with no host access. PG16+.
panels.title: Read Throughput

panels.description: Cluster-wide bytes/sec written by PostgreSQL (pg_stat_io write_bytes) across backend types - checkpointer, background writer, and regular backends. Sustained high writes from 'client backend' (rather than checkpointer/bgwriter) can mean backends are forced to flush dirty buffers themselves under pressure, which hurts query latency; ideally most writes come from the checkpointer and background writer. Correlate with 'Write & Extend Throughput by Backend Type' to see who is writing. PG16+.
panels.title: Write Throughput

panels.description: Buffer eviction rate broken down by backend_type (client backend, autovacuum, background writer, checkpointer, etc.). Tells you WHO is causing shared_buffers churn. Evictions dominated by 'client backend' are the concerning case - regular queries are fighting over the cache and evicting each other's pages. Heavy autovacuum evictions usually indicate large vacuum/analyze passes. Use with the aggregate 'Eviction Rate' KPI to attribute cache pressure to a workload. PG16+.
panels.title: Buffer Evictions by Backend Type

panels.description: Buffer eviction rate broken down by IO context: 'normal' (regular shared_buffers activity), 'bulkread' (large sequential scans using a ring buffer), 'bulkwrite' (COPY / CREATE TABLE AS), and 'vacuum'. High evictions in the 'normal' context signal a genuine shared_buffers shortage; high 'bulkread'/'bulkwrite' evictions are expected during big scans or bulk loads and are less alarming because they use small ring buffers by design. This split helps you decide whether to tune shared_buffers or just accept a transient bulk operation. PG16+.
panels.title: Buffer Evictions by Context

panels.description: Buffer cache hit ratio split by backend_type. Lets you see whether a specific class of work is cache-starved - for example a low ratio for 'client backend' points at user queries missing cache, while a low ratio for 'autovacuum worker' just reflects vacuum scanning cold pages (normal). Focus on the client-backend line for user-facing latency. Compare against the aggregate 'Buffer Cache Hit Ratio' KPI. PG16+.

targets.expr: sum(rate(pg_stat_io_hits_total{job="$job", instance="$instance"}[5m])) by (backend_type) / clamp_min(sum(rate(pg_stat_io_hits_total{job="$job", instance="$instance"}[5m])) by (backend_type) + sum(rate(pg_stat_io_reads_total{job="$job", instance="$instance"}[5m])) by (backend_type), 1)

panels.title: Cache Hit Ratio by Backend Type

panels.description: Absolute rates of buffer cache hits vs. disk reads (pg_stat_io hits and reads), side by side. Unlike the ratio panels, this shows magnitude: a 98% hit ratio can still mean a lot of disk reads if total volume is huge. A rising 'reads' line is what drives I/O load and latency. Spikes here that line up with query slowdowns confirm the issue is cache misses / physical I/O rather than CPU or locking. PG16+.
panels.title: Cache Hits vs Disk Reads

panels.description: Read bytes/sec split by backend_type. Attributes read I/O to its source: 'client backend' (user queries), 'autovacuum worker' (vacuum/analyze), background writer / checkpointer, or parallel workers. A read-heavy autovacuum is expected and usually fine; sustained heavy reads from client backends indicate the working set does not fit in cache. Use this to decide whether high 'Read Throughput' is user load or maintenance. PG16+.
panels.title: Read Throughput by Backend Type

panels.description: Write and relation-extend bytes/sec by backend_type. 'extend' is space added to grow tables/indexes (inserts, bulk loads). Ideally most plain writes come from checkpointer / background writer; large write volume from 'client backend' means backends are flushing dirty pages under pressure, which slows queries - a hint to tune checkpoint settings or shared_buffers. Heavy 'extend' by client backends is normal during ingestion / bulk load. PG16+.
panels.title: Write & Extend Throughput by Backend Type

panels.description: Average time per read operation by backend_type (read_time / reads). This is storage read latency measured from inside PostgreSQL - invaluable on managed services where you have no host iostat. Requires track_io_timing = on (otherwise this is empty/zero). Rising latency, especially for 'client backend', directly explains slow queries and usually points at the storage layer (saturated disk/EBS, noisy neighbor, throttled IOPS). Compare backend types to separate user-facing slowness from maintenance I/O. PG16+.
panels.title: Average Read Latency by Backend Type

panels.description: Average time per write and per fsync operation (write_time / writes, fsync_time / fsyncs). fsync latency is a sensitive indicator of storage durability cost - if it climbs, checkpoints and commits slow down and latency spikes cluster-wide. Requires track_io_timing = on. High fsync latency often means the underlying storage is saturated or its write cache is disabled/flushing; high write latency points at general disk pressure. Especially useful on RDS/Aurora with no host access. PG16+.
panels.title: Average Write & Fsync Latency

panels.description: Rate of buffer 'reuses' by IO context (pg_stat_io.reuses). Reuses happen when an operation using a ring buffer (large sequential scans = 'bulkread', COPY = 'bulkwrite', 'vacuum') recycles a buffer within its private ring instead of evicting from the main pool - this is by design and PROTECTS shared_buffers from being blown out by one big scan. So high reuses during bulk operations are a good sign (the ring buffer is doing its job), not a problem. Mainly informational context for the eviction panels. PG16+.
panels.title: Buffer Reuses by Context

panels.description: Rate of writebacks and fsyncs by backend_type (pg_stat_io.writebacks / fsyncs). Writebacks are hints to the OS to flush dirty pages to disk; fsyncs force durability. These are normal checkpoint / background-writer activity. A surge in fsyncs from 'client backend' suggests backends are doing their own durability work under pressure (often paired with high write throughput and latency). Use alongside 'Average Write & Fsync Latency' to judge whether the storage is keeping up. PG16+.
panels.title: Writebacks & Fsyncs

panels.description: A right-now snapshot (bar gauge) of eviction rate broken down by both backend_type and context together, so you can instantly spot the single biggest source of shared_buffers churn - for example 'client backend / normal' glowing red means user queries are thrashing the cache and you should look at shared_buffers or a heavy scan, whereas 'autovacuum worker / vacuum' is expected background work. Green is quiet; yellow/orange/red mark escalating pressure. The most actionable at-a-glance view of the eviction time series above. PG16+.
panels.title: Current Eviction Rate by Backend Type / Context

panels.description: The top 20 sequences by how much of their range they have consumed (last_value / max_value), across all monitored databases. Only sequences at or above --sequences.min-ratio (default 0.5) are exported, so a healthy young database shows nothing here - that is good. Each series is one sequence: green (under 70%) = fine, yellow (70-90%) = plan a migration, red (90% or more) = act now, the backing column is close to overflowing and INSERTs will start failing cluster-wide with 'nextval: reached maximum value of sequence'. The usual fix for an int4 (max about 2.1 billion) or int2 sequence is to migrate the owning column to bigint. Use the schemaname/sequencename/datname labels to find exactly which one to fix.
panels.title: Sequences by % Consumed

panels.description: The single fullest sequence in the cluster, as a fraction of its maximum value (last_value / max_value). At 100% the next INSERT that calls nextval() on it fails with 'nextval: reached maximum value of sequence' and writes to that table stop. Green (< 70%) = healthy; yellow (70-90%) = start planning; red (>= 90%) = migrate the backing column to a wider type (e.g. int4 -> bigint) now. Only sequences at or above --sequences.min-ratio (default 0.5) are exported, so an empty or low panel means nothing is near exhaustion, which is good.
panels.title: Max Sequence % Consumed

panels.description: How many sequences have already consumed >= 90% of their range. Each one is close to hard-failing every INSERT that calls nextval() on it (cluster-wide 'reached maximum value of sequence' errors). 0 (green) = nothing critical; >= 1 (red) = act now: find the sequence in the 'Sequences by % Consumed' table to the left and either widen its column (int4 -> bigint) or, if it is safe to do so, reset it. This is the actionable companion to 'Max Sequence % Consumed'.
panels.title: Sequences ≥ 90% Consumed
Sequence Exhaustion (requires --collector.sequences)
panels.description: Sequences approaching their maximum value. Only sequences at or above --sequences.min-ratio are exported.

In-memory hit ratio for each SLRU cache (subtransaction, multixact, commit-log/CLOG, etc.) from pg_stat_slru. These are small fixed-size caches that track transaction bookkeeping. Green (over 99%) is normal. A ratio dropping into yellow/red - especially for the 'subtrans' or 'multixact' caches - means PostgreSQL is repeatedly reading SLRU pages from disk, the classic signature of a subtransaction/savepoint storm (deeply nested SAVEPOINTs, ORMs, or SELECT ... FOR UPDATE with many savepoints) or heavy multixact row-locking. That causes sudden cluster-wide slowdowns that are near-impossible to diagnose without this panel. If it drops, check 'SLRU Disk Reads by Cache' to see which cache, and reduce savepoint/subtransaction usage.
panels.title: SLRU Cache Hit Ratio by Cache

panels.description: Rate of SLRU pages read from disk per cache (pg_stat_slru.blks_read). 0 or near-0 is healthy - it means the SLRU bookkeeping caches are serving from memory. A sustained spike on the 'subtrans' cache is the smoking gun for a subtransaction/savepoint storm; a spike on 'multixact' points to heavy shared row locking (many sessions locking the same rows via FOR SHARE / FOR UPDATE plus savepoints). Either can stall the whole cluster while every backend waits on SLRU I/O. Correlate the spiking cache name here with application behavior (nested transactions, ORM savepoints) and the 'SLRU Cache Hit Ratio' panel.
panels.title: SLRU Disk Reads by Cache (rate)

panels.description: Rate of SLRU buffer writes and flushes per cache (pg_stat_slru.blks_written / flushes). This is normal background activity as transaction and multixact state is persisted. Watch for large sustained increases that track with 'SLRU Disk Reads' - together they indicate SLRU pressure (subtransaction or multixact churn) rather than a quiet, memory-resident workload. Mostly a supporting signal; the hit-ratio and disk-read panels are the primary alerts.
panels.title: SLRU Writes & Flushes (rate)

panels.description: Rate of SLRU segment truncations and newly zeroed pages per cache (pg_stat_slru.truncates / blks_zeroed). Truncates happen as old transaction state ages out (e.g. after VACUUM advances horizons); blks_zeroed counts fresh SLRU pages being initialized as transaction IDs / multixacts are consumed. Steady low values are normal. A rising blks_zeroed rate on 'multixact' or 'subtrans' signals rapid consumption of those ID spaces - another angle on subtransaction/multixact storms. Supporting detail for the hit-ratio and disk-read panels.
panels.title: SLRU Truncates & Zeroed (rate)
SLRU Cache (requires --collector.slru)
panels.description: PostgreSQL SLRU cache efficiency per cache area (PG >= 13).

Per-mode host non-idle CPU accounting as a fraction of total capacity, averaged directly across the per-core pg_system_cpu_seconds_total{cpu,mode} series (Linux /proc/stat, FreeBSD kern.cp_times). idle is excluded so the stack exposes useful non-idle modes instead of always filling to 100%. user/nice/system/irq/softirq represent executed CPU; iowait is time associated with outstanding I/O and steal is capacity taken by the hypervisor. Both remain visible as pressure diagnostics. The raw series keep the cpu label for per-core drill-down. Requires --collector.system on a host co-located with PostgreSQL (not RDS/Aurora).
panels.title: CPU Utilization by Mode

panels.description: Total host non-idle fraction, calculated by summing the per-mode rates averaged across exactly the CPU series being charted. A value near 1.0 means the logical CPUs spent almost all accounted time in non-idle modes, regardless of core count. Use CPU Utilization by Mode to distinguish executed work from iowait or hypervisor steal; a high value alone does not prove the machine is CPU-bound. Requires --collector.system on a co-located host.
panels.title: CPU Busy (normalized across cores)

panels.description: 1/5/15-minute host load average (pg_system_load1/5/15, Linux). Compare against pg_system_cpu_cores: a load persistently above the core count means the run queue is backing up (CPU and/or I/O saturation). Reported as 0 on platforms where the OS does not expose load average.
panels.title: Load Average

panels.description: Host memory and swap in bytes (pg_system_memory_*, pg_system_swap_*). Compute memory pressure as (total - available) / total, which is accurate on Linux. Note: on FreeBSD/Windows sysinfo reports available == free, so 'mem available' understates reclaimable cache there. Rising 'swap used' on a database host is a red flag — PostgreSQL working set is being paged out. Requires --collector.system on a co-located host.
panels.title: Memory & Swap Usage

panels.description: Per-core non-idle fraction for every logical CPU, calculated by summing that CPU's non-idle mode rates from pg_system_cpu_seconds_total. One line per core (cpu 0, cpu 1, ...), each 0-100%; a value near 1.0 means almost all accounted time was non-idle during the interval. Use CPU Utilization by Mode to distinguish executed work from iowait or steal. This panel reveals a hot or pinned core that the host aggregate can hide. Requires --collector.system on a co-located host.
panels.title: Per-CPU Utilization

panels.description: CPU consumed by the whole postgres* process group on the host, as CPU-cores (rate of pg_system_process_group_cpu_seconds_total). A value of 1.0 means the group is keeping one logical core fully busy on average; divide by pg_system_cpu_cores for the busy fraction of the box. Answers "is PostgreSQL itself eating the machine, or a noisy neighbour?". Requires --collector.system on a co-located host (not RDS/Aurora).
panels.title: PostgreSQL Process Group CPU

panels.description: Resident memory of the whole postgres* process group (pg_system_process_group_memory_bytes). On Linux this is PSS (proportional set size) from /proc/<pid>/smaps_rollup, so PostgreSQL's shared_buffers is counted once across all backends rather than multiplied per connection - PSS requires the exporter to run as the postgres user or root, otherwise it falls back to RSS (which over-counts shared memory). On FreeBSD this is summed RSS. The right axis shows the live postgres* process count. Requires --collector.system on a co-located host (not RDS/Aurora).
panels.title: PostgreSQL Process Group Memory
Host CPU / Memory (requires --collector.system, co-located only — not RDS/Aurora)
panels.description: Host-level CPU and memory for the machine running pg_exporter, from --collector.system (Linux/FreeBSD). Reads only the OS (/proc/stat, sysctls, sysinfo) and never queries PostgreSQL. Only meaningful when the exporter is co-located with the database server; on managed services (RDS/Aurora) these numbers describe the exporter's host, not the DB.

CPU usage of the pg_exporter process, shown two ways.

**CPU % of one core** (left axis, solid) = pg_exporter_process_cpu_percent:
- Raw process CPU, exactly as ps/top/pidstat report it (100% = one full core).
- Can exceed 100% on multi-core hosts. Example: 124% = 1.24 cores of CPU in total.
- This is NOT the load of a single physical core; it is the whole process across all its threads.

**% of whole host** (right axis, dashed) = cpu_percent / pg_exporter_process_cpu_cores:
- The exporter's share of the entire machine, always 0-100% regardless of core count.
- Example: 124% on a 24-core host = 124 / 24 = 5.17% of the box.

**Typical "CPU % of one core" values:** idle 1-5%, light 5-15%, moderate 15-30%, heavy 30-50%.

**Note:** the first reading after startup is 0% (sysinfo needs two refreshes for an accurate delta).
panels.title: Exporter Process CPU Usage

panels.description:
Memory usage of the pg_exporter process.

**RSS (Resident Set Size):** Actual physical RAM used by the process
- What's counted: Code, stack, heap, shared libraries actively in RAM
- This is the "real" memory usage you care about

**VSZ (Virtual Size):** Total virtual memory allocated
- Includes: RSS + swapped memory + memory-mapped files + shared libraries
- Usually 2-10x larger than RSS (this is normal!)
- Example: RSS=45MB, VSZ=500MB is typical

**GOAL:** Stable RSS over time (no memory leaks)

**ACTIONS IF RSS INCREASING:**
1. Check for memory leaks in exporter
2. Reduce number of collectors or databases monitored
3. Check metric cardinality explosion (see Total Metrics panel)
4. Review connection pool settings

**NOTE:** VSZ growth is usually harmless - focus on RSS trends
panels.title: Exporter Memory Usage

panels.description:
Number of open file descriptors.

GOAL: Stay well below system limit (usually 1024 or ulimit -n)

WARNING: If this approaches the limit, the exporter will fail with 'too many open files'

ACTIONS IF HIGH:
1. Check for file descriptor leaks
2. Increase ulimit if needed
3. Review database connection pooling
panels.title: Open File Descriptors

panels.description:
Collector health timeline showing scrape status over time.

**GREEN** = Scraping successfully
**RED** = Failed scrape

**LEGEND SHOWS:**
- Collector name
- Current status (hover for details)

**WHAT TO LOOK FOR:**
- Red bars = Failed scrapes (check logs immediately)
- Gaps = Collector not running/enabled
- Flapping (red/green/red) = Intermittent issues

**ACTIONS IF RED:**
1. Check pg_exporter logs for error details
2. Verify database connectivity and permissions
3. Check required extensions (pg_stat_statements, etc.)
4. Review collector-specific requirements

**TIP:** Pair with 'Collector Scrape Duration' panel on right to see if slow collectors correlate with failures
panels.title: Collector Health Timeline

panels.description:
Time spent collecting metrics from PostgreSQL per collector.

GOAL: < 1 second per collector

Use to identify slow collectors.
panels.title: Collector Scrape Duration

panels.description:
Time elapsed since each collector last successfully scraped.

**GOAL:** < 60 seconds (should match scrape interval)

**GREEN:** Recent scrape (< 60s)
**YELLOW:** Delayed (60-120s)
**RED:** Stale/stuck (> 120s)

**SHOWS:**
- Which collectors are running on schedule
- Which collectors are stuck/stale
- How long since last successful collection

**ACTIONS IF HIGH:**
1. Check if collector is enabled
2. Look for errors in Health Timeline above
3. Check Scrape Duration on right for slow collectors
4. Review pg_exporter logs
panels.title: Time Since Last Scrape

panels.description:
Total number of metrics currently exported by pg_exporter.

GOAL: Keep cardinality under your monitoring platform limits
- Cortex/Mimir: Often have strict series limits
- Prometheus: High cardinality = high memory usage

ACTIONS IF TOO HIGH:
1. Disable unnecessary collectors
2. Filter databases (use PG_EXPORTER_EXCLUDE_DATABASES)
3. Review metric labels for high cardinality
4. Check for label explosion (many tables/databases)
panels.title: Total Metrics Exported

panels.description:
Time since pg_exporter process started.

Shows when the exporter was last restarted.
panels.title: Uptime

panels.description: pg_exporter version currently running
panels.title: Exporter Version
Exporter Self-Monitoring (enable --collector.exporter)
panels.description: Monitor pg_exporter's own performance and health. Enable with --collector.exporter flag.

title: PostgreSQL Monitoring Dashboard
description: PostgreSQL Performance Monitoring Dashboard This dashboard provides comprehensive monitoring for PostgreSQL databases using pg_exporter metrics. KEY SECTIONS: - Instance Information: PostgreSQL version, settings, and uptime - Critical Alerts: Connection pool utilization and performance warnings - Connection Analysis: Active connections, idle transactions, and connection age - Query Performance: Top queries by execution time, calls, and I/O (requires pg_stat_statements extension) - Cache & I/O: Buffer cache hit ratios, shared buffers, and I/O operations - Locks & Blocking: Lock monitoring and blocking query detection - Vacuum & Maintenance: Autovacuum activity, table bloat, and maintenance statistics - WAL: WAL throughput, record activity, buffer pressure, and FPI ratio - Replication: lag, role, and max replication lag bytes (requires replication collector) - Table Statistics: DML operations and table-level activity - Database Size: Database growth and size trends REQUIREMENTS: - PostgreSQL 14+ - pg_stat_statements extension enabled for query performance metrics - Appropriate monitoring user permissions For more information, visit: https://github.com/nbari/pg_exporter
